Algeria.text: Algeria's group stage exit came with a bang rather than a whimper: a 3-3 thriller against Austria that lurched through leads and equalisers before Mahrez pulled them level in the third minute of stoppage time, only for Kalajdzic to snatch it back for Austria at 90+6 — a frantic finish that summed up a tournament passage just unconvincing enough to leave them scrapping for a third-place berth. Switzerland await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT, a coin-flip encounter between two sides of broadly comparable continental pedigree, though Algeria's defensive fragility — seven goals conceded in the group — will give the Swiss genuine encouragement. Survive that, and Colombia or Ghana could follow in the Round of 16, with Argentina a looming possibility in the quarterfinal should the Fennecs' run extend that far.

Argentina.text: Argentina swept through Group J without dropping a point, and their 3-1 dismissal of Jordan was a composed, controlled affair — two goals before half-time putting the contest to bed, Messi adding a third late on after Jordan had briefly threatened to make a game of it. Cape Verde await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT, and the defending champions start as heavy favorites against a side whose tournament ambitions outpace their resources. Should Argentina advance as expected, Australia or Egypt loom in the Round of 16, before the draw opens toward a potential quarterfinal against Colombia or Switzerland — the kind of opposition that would offer a genuine examination of whether this squad retains the steel of its 2022 vintage.

Australia.text: A goalless draw with Paraguay to close the group stage — functional rather than fluent, Australia doing enough to bank the point that secured second place without ever threatening to make it a statement. They meet Egypt in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT, a tie that carries genuine expectation of an Australian passage: Egypt are a capable side but this is a matchup the Socceroos will approach as favourites, however much that stalemate against Paraguay suggests rust remains in the attacking machinery. Win that, and Argentina could await in the Round of 16 — a test of an entirely different magnitude — with Colombia or Switzerland potentially lurking in the quarterfinal beyond.

Austria.text: Austria scraped through Group J in the most dramatic fashion: 3-3 against Algeria, a match that swung twice before Kalajdzic's equaliser deep into stoppage time spared them what would have been a chastening late elimination. The manner of it — nearly capitulating to Mahrez twice in the final half-hour — will give the coaching staff serious pause. Spain await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT, and that is a formidable assignment by any measure; Austria will need to be considerably more composed than they were here if they are to cause one of the tournament's genuine heavyweights any difficulty. Survive that, and Croatia or Portugal could follow in the Round of 16.

Belgium.text: Belgium closed the group stage with something approaching a procession: Trossard struck twice before the hour, Bruyne and Lukaku added gloss in the final stages, and a late flurry left New Zealand thoroughly dismantled, 5-1. That kind of emphatic, front-to-back performance announces a side hitting its rhythm at the right moment. Senegal await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT — a genuine test rather than a formality, the Africans capable of pressing Belgium's defensive lines, though the Red Devils enter as clear favourites. Should they advance, Portugal or Spain could loom in the quarterfinal, with France or Germany conceivable semifinal obstacles on the path to the final.

Bosnia & Herzegovina.text: Bosnia & Herzegovina closed out the group stage with purpose, putting Qatar to the sword 3-1 in a controlled, largely dominant performance — two goals in five first-half minutes settled the contest well before Mahmic added gloss late on, Qatar's consolation barely interrupting the rhythm. That was enough to squeeze through as one of the best third-placed teams, and the reward is a Round of 32 date with host nation USA on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT — a formidable assignment against a side carrying the weight of a home crowd and genuine tournament ambition, with Bosnia the considerable underdogs. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal await in the Round of 16, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Portugal or Spain that would demand something close to a perfect performance.

Brazil.text: Brazil scraped past Japan in the round of 32 in a match that had more drama than the Seleção would have wanted — Sano's opener forced them to chase the game, Casemiro levelled midway through the second half, and it took a Martinelli goal deep in stoppage time to settle it. Next up is either Ivory Coast or Norway in the round of 16 on Sun Jul 5 13:00 PT, a winnable tie for the five-time world champions but one that demands greater composure than they showed against Japan. Should Brazil advance, England or Mexico could await in the quarterfinal — and beyond that, the draw opens up into genuinely dangerous territory, with Argentina lurking as a possible semifinal opponent.

Canada.text: Canada edged past South Africa in the most nerve-shredding of fashions, Stephen Eustáquio's goal deep into stoppage time the difference in a 1-0 win that flattered neither side for large stretches. The Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT brings either Morocco or the Netherlands — the latter a formidable European side that would represent a genuine examination of Canada's quality, while Morocco's tournament pedigree makes this no soft landing regardless. Survive that, and a quarterfinal pool featuring France or Germany looms on Thu Jul 9 13:00 PT, the kind of opposition that would demand a step up Canada has yet to convincingly demonstrate.

Cape Verde.text: Cape Verde navigated Group H without conceding a goal, though three successive draws — the last a goalless stalemate against Saudi Arabia — speak to a side that defends with discipline but has yet to impose itself in attack. They qualified, just, on the slimmest of margins. Now comes the severest of examinations: Argentina in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 15:00 PT, a fixture that, on almost any reading, is a mountain the Blue Sharks are expected not to climb. Survive that, and Australia or Egypt await in the Round of 16 — followed, potentially, by Colombia or Algeria in the quarterfinal, opponents whose stature would still demand something close to the perfect game.

Colombia.text: A goalless draw against Portugal to close the group stage looks more like shrewd game management than a stumble — Colombia had already secured top spot, and a controlled, combative 0-0 against one of Europe's heavyweights is no embarrassment. They enter the knockout rounds as Group K winners with a seven-point haul and a positive goal difference, carrying genuine momentum. Ghana await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, a winnable assignment for a Colombian side that will start as clear favorites against a team of considerably lower continental standing. Should they advance, Algeria or Switzerland loom in the Round of 16 on Tue Jul 7 13:00 PT, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Argentina — a fixture that would announce Colombia's tournament in the loudest possible terms.

Croatia.text: Croatia edged past Ghana 2-1 in a nervy group finale — Sucic's first-half opener looked sufficient until Luckassen levelled with seventeen minutes remaining, before Vlasic's late intervention rescued the points and confirmed second place in Group L. It was the kind of win that flattered the scoreline only slightly; Croatia did enough without ever looking commanding. Portugal await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, and that is a genuine examination: Martínez's side carry the weight of expectation and individual quality that makes Croatia's task formidable, though this is a team with a proven habit of outlasting more fancied opponents in knockout football. Survive that, and either Spain or Austria lurk in the Round of 16, with the prospect of Belgium or the United States potentially standing between Croatia and a semifinal place.

Curaçao.text: Curaçao closed their group stage campaign with a 0-2 defeat to Ivory Coast, Nicolas Pépé striking early and then again just past the hour to make it a controlled, comfortable evening for the Elephants. The result confirmed what the tournament had largely borne out across three matches: Curaçao, plucky debutants on the greatest stage, were outgunned at this level. They exit with a single point, a goal difference of minus nine, and the distinction of having competed — if not quite threatened — against top-tier opposition in Group E.

Czech Republic.text: Czech Republic's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony — a 0-3 defeat to Mexico that was effectively settled inside the opening quarter-hour of the second half, Chávez and Quiñones striking within six minutes of each other before Fidalgo's stoppage-time finish applied a flattering coat of finality to what had already been a comfortable Mexican evening. The Czechs finished fourth in Group A with a single point and a goals difference of minus four, scraping through as one of the better third-placed sides before their tournament ended here, emphatically, on the wrong side of a mismatch they could never quite solve.

DR Congo.text: DR Congo trailed Uzbekistan inside ten minutes but recovered with authority, Wissa converting from the spot before adding a second deep in stoppage time either side of Mayele's strike — a composed, backs-to-the-wall response that secured their passage as one of the best third-placed sides. England await in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT, a formidable assignment by any measure: Gareth Southgate's side carry the weight of expectation, and Congo will need to reproduce their second-half resilience to cause a genuine upset. Survive that and Ecuador or Mexico stand in the way of a quarterfinal, where Brazil, Ivory Coast, or Norway could provide an even sterner examination of their credentials.

Ecuador.text: Ecuador close their group stage in genuine style, going behind inside two minutes to Sané before Angulo levelled early and Plata sealed a 2-1 victory over Germany with a second-half strike — a comeback win against one of the tournament's heavyweights that announces the Ecuadorians as a side worth respecting. They meet Mexico in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 18:00 PT, a charged derby that carries little of the routine about it; both sides know each other intimately, and this figures as a tightly contested affair with Mexico slight favourites on pedigree but Ecuador carrying real momentum. Survive that, and England or DR Congo could await in the last sixteen, with Brazil potentially looming in the quarterfinals.

Egypt.text: Egypt's group stage ended with a cautious, slightly scrappy draw against Iran — Saber struck inside five minutes only for Rezaeian to level nine minutes later, and neither side could force a winner. The Pharaohs advance in second place, points banked, but questions linger about their ability to control a match once parity is restored. Australia await in the Round of 32 on Fri Jul 3 11:00 PT, a genuinely open contest between two sides of comparable standing — Egypt will carry marginal favour but the Socceroos are no pushover, and this is no formality. Survive that, and Argentina could loom in the Round of 16, with a potential quarterfinal against Colombia or Algeria adding further intrigue to a bracket that offers jeopardy at every turn.

England.text: England closed out Group L with a controlled, if unhurried, 2-0 win over Panama — Bellingham and Kane scoring within five minutes of each other midway through the second half to settle a game that never seriously threatened to slip away. They meet DR Congo in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 09:00 PT, a fixture that, on reputation and form, England should navigate without alarm, though African sides at this stage of the tournament have long since learned not to be impressed by the Three Lions. Survive that and a Round of 16 date with Ecuador or Mexico awaits, before the draw darkens considerably in the quarterfinal, where Brazil could be standing in the way.

France.text: Dembélé dismantled Norway almost single-handedly, completing a first-half hat-trick inside 32 minutes before Doué added gloss deep in stoppage time — the sole consolation Aasgaard scored between Dembélé's first and second only underlined how emphatically France controlled the occasion. They top Group I with a perfect record and meet Sweden in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 14:00 PT, a tie that strongly favours the French; Sweden will make it competitive, but this is the kind of assignment a side in this form expects to negotiate without alarm. Should they advance through a potential Round of 16 meeting with Germany, the draw opens onto a quarterfinal where Morocco or the Netherlands could await — and either would constitute a genuine examination of whether this France vintage is built for the distance.

Germany.text: Germany closed the group stage with a stumble, conceding twice to Ecuador after Leroy Sané's lightning opener — a 2-1 defeat that flattered no one, with the winning goal arriving only in the 77th minute to confirm a genuine scare. They top Group E nonetheless, and Paraguay await in the Round of 32 on Mon Jun 29 13:30 PT: a manageable assignment on paper, though Germany will need to rediscover the fluency of their earlier wins against a South American side capable of making life uncomfortable. Beyond that, France or Sweden loom in the Round of 16, with the Netherlands, Morocco, or Canada potential quarterfinal opponents — a bracket that could demand excellence before the last four is even reached.

Ghana.text: Ghana's group stage ended on a sour note — a 1-2 defeat to Croatia in which they pulled level through Luckassen on 73 minutes only for Vlasic to restore the Croatian lead eight minutes later, a late sucker punch that dropped them to third. They survive nonetheless, into a Round of 32 appointment with Colombia on Fri Jul 3 18:30 PT, a tie that favors the South Americans on reputation but is far from settled — Ghana showed enough resilience in qualification to make this uncomfortable. Beat Colombia and Algeria or Switzerland await in the last 16, with Argentina a lurking possibility should the Black Stars reach the quarterfinal.

Haiti.text: Haiti's final group game against Morocco ended 4-2, a scoreline that flatters neither side in quite the right way — the Haitians led briefly, equalized before half-time through Isidor, yet conceded four times across a match Morocco ultimately controlled with growing authority. Two late Moroccan goals in the final stages confirmed what the second half had suggested: Haiti were outclassed and outrun. They finish bottom of Group C without a point, their tournament defined by an 8-2 aggregate against them across three matches, and exit a World Cup that exposed the gulf between their ambition and the level required.

Iran.text: Iran's group stage ends with a 1-1 draw against Egypt — a match in which they surrendered the initiative inside five minutes before Rezaeian levelled early in the second quarter, and neither side could force a winner. Three draws across three games told the full story of a tournament in which Iran were never beaten but never convincing, accumulating just enough to squeeze through as one of the best third-placed sides. They exit at the round of 32, undone not by any single defeat but by a persistent inability to turn resistance into results.

Iraq.text: Iraq's group stage ended in humiliation against Senegal, a 5-0 thrashing that was effectively settled inside the first minute and ruthlessly extended across both halves. The Africans scored through five different players, the goals arriving in waves rather than any sustained Iraqi resistance. It was the punctuation mark on a tournament Iraq would sooner forget — three defeats, twelve goals conceded, none scored, and elimination from a competition they technically advanced into as a best third-placed side, only to find the step up in class absolute and unforgiving.

Ivory Coast.text: Nicolas Pépé settled the Ivory Coast's group stage account against Curaçao with a brace — the opener inside seven minutes, the second just past the hour — a controlled, professional performance that carried no particular alarm. They face Norway in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 10:00 PT, a genuinely open contest: Norway arrive with physical menace and an attacking threat that makes this no formality, though the Elephants' quality and tournament experience give them a slight edge. Survive that, and Brazil await in the Round of 16 — a stiffer examination by any measure, and a potential quarterfinal against England would demand everything they have.

Japan.text: Japan exit the group stage with a bruising lesson from Brazil: Sano's early strike gave them the lead and genuine belief, but Casemiro's equaliser midway through the second half steadied the Seleção, and Martinelli's stoppage-time winner was a cruel, late twist that obscured how hard Japan had made it. They regroup now for a Round of 16 tie against either Ivory Coast or Norway on Sun Jul 5 13:00 PT — a more navigable assignment than what just passed, with Japan entering as moderate favourites regardless of which side comes through. Win that, and England or Mexico could await in the quarterfinal, with Argentina or Colombia potentially looming in the semifinal as the true measure of whether this side can threaten the latter stages.

Jordan.text:
Jordan's final group-stage outing against Argentina followed a familiar script: two goals down by the half-hour mark, Al-Tamari pulling one back just after the interval to briefly threaten a comeback, before Messi settled the contest with ten minutes to go. It was a respectable showing in impossible company, but the gulf in class was never seriously in doubt.

Winless across three matches and with a goal difference of minus five, Jordan nonetheless advanced as one of the best third-placed sides — only to exit at that stage, their tournament concluding without a knockout fixture to call their own.

Mexico.text: Mexico swept through Group A without conceding a single goal, and their 3-0 dismissal of Czech Republic — settled in a brisk six-minute burst after the hour before Fidalgo added gloss deep in stoppage time — had the look of a side still within themselves. They meet Ecuador in the Round of 32 on Tue Jun 30 18:00 PT, a winnable assignment against a South American side they have the quality and momentum to handle, though Ecuador will not be rolled over lightly. Survive that, and England lurk as a possible Round of 16 opponent, with Brazil potentially waiting in the quarterfinals to provide the sternest examination of whether this Mexico side is genuine contenders.

Morocco.text: Morocco came through their group finale against Haiti in a match that lurched before settling — conceding inside ten minutes and again just before half-time, yet Hakimi and Saibari struck either side of the interval to restore order, and two late goals put a commanding gloss on what had been an uncomfortable afternoon. They face the Netherlands in the Round of 32 on Mon Jun 29 18:00 PT, a serious examination: the Dutch carry the weight of expectation that attaches to a major European side, and Morocco, for all their quality, enter as underdogs whose compact, combative style makes them awkward rather than comfortable opponents. Survive that, and Canada await in the Round of 16 on Sat Jul 4 10:00 PT — a more navigable proposition on paper. The quarterfinal draw could then deliver France or Germany, which would represent the Atlas Lions at their most defiant.

Netherlands.text: Netherlands closed the group stage in assured fashion, leading Tunisia inside seven minutes through an own goal and Brobbey's quick follow-up, then weathering a brief Mastouri equaliser after the break before Hecke restored the margin for a comfortable 3-1 victory. Morocco await in the Round of 32 on Mon Jun 29 18:00 PT — a genuine test against a well-organised side that has troubled European heavyweights before, though the Dutch, as group winners with momentum and firepower, carry the favourite's burden. Should they advance, Canada stand in the way in the Round of 16, and a quarterfinal could yet produce a marquee collision with France or Germany.

New Zealand.text: Belgium dismantled New Zealand with something close to contempt, Trossard scoring either side of half-time before De Bruyne and Lukaku twisted the knife — the All Whites' consolation on 84 minutes a cosmetic gesture against a 5-1 final score. New Zealand's tournament is over: they finish fourth in Group G, their single point earned somewhere earlier in the group stage, the gap in class against Europe's elite ultimately impossible to bridge.

Norway.text: France dismantled Norway with ruthless efficiency, Ousmane Dembélé completing a first-half hat-trick inside 32 minutes to leave the Scandinavians chasing shadows — Aasgaard's brief reply purely cosmetic before Doué added a fourth in stoppage time. That humbling exit from the group stage sets a sobering tone heading into Tuesday Jun 30 10:00 PT against Ivory Coast in the Round of 32, a winnable tie on paper but one that demands a far sturdier defensive showing than Norway produced against Les Bleus. Survive that, and Brazil await in the Round of 16 — a monumental examination — with England or Mexico potentially lurking in the quarterfinal beyond.

Panama.text: Panama's group stage exit was confirmed with a 0-2 defeat to England, a result that flattered neither side in the opening hour before Bellingham and Kane struck in quick succession midway through the second half to put the tie beyond doubt. Three losses, no points, and a four-goal deficit across Group L tells the full story of a tournament in which Panama never found their footing at this level. They depart having shown that the step up to a 48-team World Cup brought them no easier path to the knockout rounds.

Paraguay.text: A goalless draw against Australia rounded off Paraguay's group stage with the kind of tidy, functional stalemate that keeps a tournament alive without inspiring any great confidence — they advance as a best third-placed side rather than through any particular statement of intent. Germany await in the Round of 32 on Mon Jun 29 13:30 PT, and this is a formidable test by any measure: the four-time world champions carry the weight of expectation into every knockout tie, and Paraguay will need to produce something well beyond their group-stage showing to trouble them. Survive that, and France or Sweden could follow in the Round of 16, with the likes of the Netherlands or Morocco lurking in the quarterfinal — a path that demands Paraguay find another gear entirely.

Portugal.text: A goalless draw with Colombia to close the group stage — functional rather than inspiring, a match that neither side seemed particularly eager to win — left Portugal finishing second in Group K despite a positive overall record and a handsome goal difference built earlier in the campaign. Croatia await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 16:00 PT, a fixture that carries genuine edge: this is no routine assignment, with Luka Modrić's generation having inflicted pain on Portugal before, though the Croatians' own advancing years make this closer to a coin-flip than a banana skin. Should Portugal advance, Spain loom as a potential Round of 16 opponent — a derby of the Iberian giants — and France or Germany could conceivably be waiting in the semifinal.

Qatar.text: Qatar's group stage exit arrived with little ceremony, a 1-3 defeat to Bosnia & Herzegovina that laid bare the limitations of a side that rarely threatened to trouble the tournament's upper tier. Behind by two goals before half-time following strikes from Alajbegovic and Abunada, Al-Haydos pulled one back on the stroke of the interval to offer a fleeting flicker of hope, only for Mahmic to extinguish it in the 80th minute. The hosts of 2022 finished bottom of Group B and depart with a single point to their name, a tournament record that reflects, honestly enough, where this squad currently stands among the world's best.

Saudi Arabia.text: Saudi Arabia's group stage ended with a goalless draw against Cape Verde that said little for either side — a flat, scoreless contest that confirmed the Green Falcons' limitations rather than dispelling them. Across three matches they managed a single goal, earned two draws, and finished fourth in Group H, advancing only by the grace of the best third-placed team standings. Their tournament ended there: not in a blaze of controversy or misfortune, but in the quiet arithmetic of a side that rarely threatened and ultimately ran out of road.

Scotland.text: Brazil ended Scotland's tournament with something close to contempt — Júnior struck twice before half-time, the second a stoppage-time flourish that summarised the gulf in class, and Cunha added a third after the interval to make it a thorough, joyless afternoon. Scotland had squeezed through as one of the better third-placed sides, a minor achievement rendered hollow by the manner of their exit. They leave the 2026 World Cup having won once in three matches, outscored four goals to one, and ultimately exposed by the first genuine elite opposition they faced.

Senegal.text: Senegal closed the group stage with a statement: a 5-0 demolition of Iraq that began inside four minutes and never relented, Gueye's brace in the second half underlining a ruthlessness that had been absent earlier in the tournament. That form, however, counts for little when Belgium arrive on Wed Jul 1 13:00 PT in the Round of 32 — a side carrying considerably more pedigree and expectation, making this a stern test for a team that scraped through as one of the better third-placed sides. Survive that, and Bosnia & Herzegovina or the host United States await in the Round of 16, before the draw opens toward a potential quarterfinal against Spain or Portugal.

South Africa.text: A stoppage-time strike from Eustáquio condemned South Africa to a 1-0 defeat by Canada in the Round of 32 — a cruel ending to a match they may well feel they deserved more from, edged out only by a goal that arrived when the draw seemed the likeliest outcome. Their tournament is over, then, not with a collapse but with a wound: eliminated in the last breath of normal time, denied a place in the last sixteen by the narrowest and most punishing of margins.

South Korea.text: South Korea's group stage ended with a 0-1 defeat to South Africa, Maseko's 63rd-minute strike enough to send the Taeguk Warriors out on the wrong note — a flat, uninspiring exit that offered little in mitigation. They had scrapped through as one of the best third-placed sides, but the tournament caught up with them in Atlanta, and a side that never quite found cohesion or cutting edge departed at the first hurdle, their World Cup lasting exactly three matches and yielding just three points.

Spain.text: Spain topped Group H without conceding a goal, and their 1-0 win over Uruguay — settled by Baena's first-half strike and protected with characteristic defensive composure — confirmed them as one of the tournament's most controlled sides. Austria await in the Round of 32 on Thu Jul 2 12:00 PT, a tie Spain enter as clear favourites against a side without comparable pedigree at this level, though Austrian organisation will demand continued precision rather than complacency. Should they advance, Croatia or Portugal could appear in the Round of 16 — either a genuinely dangerous proposition — before the bracket potentially delivers France or Germany in the semifinal.

Sweden.text: Sweden scraped through Group F in third place after a nervy 1-1 draw with Japan — Maeda's opener on 56 minutes put them behind before Elanga equalised six minutes later, a result that flattered neither side but proved just enough to keep them alive. The reward is a Round of 32 date with France on Tue Jun 30 14:00 PT, as daunting a draw as the last 32 can produce: Sweden arrive as clear underdogs against one of the tournament's genuine contenders, needing a performance well above their group-stage ceiling to advance. Survive that, and Germany or Paraguay await in the Round of 16 — with the Netherlands a looming possibility beyond.

Switzerland.text: Switzerland controlled the group stage finale against Canada with a measured authority — two goals in eleven second-half minutes from Vargas and Manzambi put them firmly in command, and Jonathan David's late consolation did little more than trim the scoreline. The Swiss top Group B and carry genuine momentum into the Round of 32 against Algeria on Thu Jul 2 20:00 PT, a tie that favors them on reputation and form, though Algerian sides have never been straightforward to dismiss. Survive that and a Round of 16 meeting with Colombia or Ghana awaits, before a potential quarterfinal collision with Argentina that would be the tournament's sternest test of Swiss mettle yet.

Tunisia.text:
There are a few data issues worth flagging before writing this blurb.

The `status` field says Tunisia "advanced as a best third-placed team out of Group F," but `finish` says "4th place," `eliminated` is true, and the record shows 0 points from three losses — a team that cannot have advanced. The `last_match` also has an apparent error: "Skhiri 3'" and "Hecke 62'" appear in `opponent_goals` (Netherlands), but Skhiri and Hecke are not Dutch players — Skhiri is Tunisian, and Hecke is associated with Belgian/Dutch club football but the own-goal or misattribution here is unclear. Most strikingly, one of Tunisia's listed `opponent_goals` is likely a Tunisian own goal or a data-entry mix-up.

I don't want to invent a clean narrative around contradictory facts. Could you clarify:

1. Did Tunisia finish 4th and exit, or did they genuinely advance?
2. Are the goal attributions correct — specifically, were Skhiri and Hecke own goals credited to Netherlands, or are they misplaced in the wrong scorer list?

Once the brief is consistent, I'll write the blurb accurately.

Turkey.text: Turkey's group stage exit arrived with the cruel drama their tournament deserved — a 3-2 win over the United States that was ultimately rendered meaningless in the standings. The Americans struck first through Trusty inside three minutes, yet Güler and Yilmaz turned things around before half-time, only for Berhalter to level early in the second half; Ayhan's stoppage-time header deep into added time sealed the points but not progress. Turkey finished fourth in Group D, their one victory coming too late and too narrow in goal difference to carry them through, and so a tournament of intermittent promise ends without a knockout stage to show for it.

USA.text: The United States finished top of Group D despite a bruising final group outing against Turkey — Trusty's third-minute opener gave brief cause for optimism, but three Turkish goals, the last a gut-punch deep in stoppage time, confirmed a 3-2 defeat that carried the sting of a cautionary tale even as the overall group record held up. The hosts meet Bosnia & Herzegovina in the Round of 32 on Wed Jul 1 17:00 PT, a tie that favors the Americans on paper but one their leaky defensive shape must resolve quickly against a side capable of punishing the same vulnerabilities Turkey exposed. Survive that, and Belgium or Senegal await in the Round of 16 — after which a quarterfinal draw from the bracket of Austria, Croatia, Portugal, and Spain would represent a genuine examination of just how far this team can go on home soil.

Uruguay.text: Uruguay's group stage ended with a narrow 0-1 defeat to Spain, Baena's first-half strike proving the solitary difference in a contest that felt tighter than the scoreline suggests — a game Uruguay never quite unlocked despite staying in it until the final whistle. They leave the tournament having advanced as one of the best third-placed teams, only to find no road beyond the group: two draws and a single defeat across three matches tell the story of a side that ground out points without ever imposing themselves, their tournament concluding in the group stage of World Cup 2026.

Uzbekistan.text: Uzbekistan signed off from their maiden World Cup with a 1-3 defeat to DR Congo that told a familiar story: an early Shomurodov strike offered fleeting promise, before the Congolese took control with a penalty from Wissa, a Mayele effort, and a late third that confirmed the margin was entirely deserved. They finish bottom of Group K without a point, having conceded eleven goals across three matches — a brutal education at football's highest level. Central Asia's standard-bearers depart having shown courage simply in qualifying, but the gap in class exposed here gives the next generation a clear-eyed reckoning to build from.
